primary_actions: Pressure redistribution (reposition every 2 hours)
Apply transparent film or skin barrier/protectant
Keep skin clean and dry — use pH-balanced cleanser
Apply moisture barrier cream to protect intact skin

dressings.name: Transparent Film Dressing
dressings.reason: Protects intact skin, allows visual monitoring without removal

dressings.name: Skin Barrier Cream
dressings.reason: Prevents moisture damage and friction on intact skin

Stage 1.nutrition: Ensure adequate protein intake (1.25-1.5 g/kg/day) and hydration
Stage 1.monitoring: Reassess every shift; if redness does not resolve in 72 hours, escalate
Stage 1.referral: None required unless non-resolving after 72 hours

primary_actions: Maintain moist wound healing environment
Cleanse with normal saline or non-cytotoxic wound cleanser
Apply moisture-retentive dressing
Pressure redistribution — support surface if not already in place
Protect periwound skin with barrier product

dressings.name: Hydrocolloid Dressing
dressings.reason: Creates moist environment, promotes autolytic debridement of minor slough

dressings.name: Foam Dressing (thin)
dressings.reason: Absorbent for light-to-moderate exudate, cushioning protection

dressings.name: Hydrogel (if dry)
dressings.reason: Adds moisture to dry wound beds, rehydrates tissue

Stage 2.nutrition: High-protein diet (1.25-1.5 g/kg/day), vitamin C and zinc supplementation
Stage 2.monitoring: Reassess every 1-3 days; measure wound dimensions weekly
Stage 2.referral: Wound care nurse consult if no improvement in 2 weeks

primary_actions: Wound bed preparation — debride slough/necrotic tissue
Cleanse with normal saline irrigation (gentle pressure)
Apply absorbent dressing for exudate management
Fill dead space with packing (alginate rope or hydrofiber)
Assess for undermining or tunneling
Evaluate for wound infection (odor, warmth, erythema, purulence)
Pressure redistribution with specialty support surface

dressings.name: Alginate Dressing
dressings.reason: Highly absorbent, forms gel with exudate, ideal for deep wounds

dressings.name: Foam Dressing (thick)
dressings.reason: Secondary cover over alginate, absorbs excess drainage

dressings.name: Hydrofiber / Gelling Fiber
dressings.reason: Locks exudate into fiber structure, manages heavy drainage

dressings.name: Silver Antimicrobial Dressing (if infected)
dressings.reason: Broad-spectrum antimicrobial action for infected or at-risk wounds

debridement.method: Autolytic or sharp debridement as indicated
debridement.details: If slough covers >30% of wound bed, consider sharp debridement by trained clinician. For smaller amounts, autolytic debridement with hydrogel under occlusive dressing.

Stage 3.nutrition: High-protein diet (1.5-2.0 g/kg/day), caloric supplementation, vitamins A, C, zinc
Stage 3.monitoring: Reassess at every dressing change (every 1-3 days); weekly wound measurement
Stage 3.referral: Wound care specialist consult; consider surgical evaluation

primary_actions: URGENT: Surgical consult for possible operative debridement
Comprehensive wound bed preparation
Aggressive debridement of all necrotic tissue
Manage heavy exudate with absorbent dressing systems
Negative-Pressure Wound Therapy (NPWT/VAC) consideration
Rule out osteomyelitis (bone infection) — may need imaging/biopsy
Assess for systemic infection (labs: WBC, CRP, blood cultures)
Specialty pressure redistribution surface (low-air-loss or alternating pressure)

dressings.name: Alginate Packing (for deep cavities)
dressings.reason: Fills dead space, absorbs heavy exudate, promotes granulation

dressings.name: Negative-Pressure Wound Therapy (NPWT)
dressings.reason: Removes exudate, reduces edema, promotes granulation in large wounds

dressings.name: Silver Antimicrobial Dressing
dressings.reason: Critical infection risk — antimicrobial barrier essential

dressings.name: Collagen Dressing (once clean)
dressings.reason: Promotes granulation tissue formation in clean wound beds

debridement.details: Stage 4 wounds with significant necrosis require aggressive debridement. Stable dry eschar on heels is an EXCEPTION — do not debride unless infection is suspected.

Stage 4.nutrition: Aggressive nutritional support: 2.0+ g/kg/day protein, caloric supplementation, multivitamin
Stage 4.monitoring: Daily assessment; wound measurement at each dressing change
Stage 4.referral: Surgical consult REQUIRED; wound care team; nutritionist; consider IV antibiotics

primary_actions: Cannot stage until wound bed is visible — debridement first priority
Soften eschar with hydrogel if sharp debridement not immediately available
Do NOT debride stable, dry, intact eschar on heels without clinical reason
Assess for underlying infection beneath eschar/slough
Once debrided, re-assess and stage appropriately

dressings.name: Hydrogel (to soften eschar)
dressings.reason: Rehydrates and softens dry eschar to facilitate autolytic debridement

dressings.name: Honey-based Dressing (medical grade)
dressings.reason: Osmotic debridement, antimicrobial, promotes moist environment

debridement.method: Determine appropriate debridement method based on clinical assessment
debridement.details: Unstageable wounds need debridement to reveal the true wound bed. Method depends on patient status, wound location, and clinician expertise. EXCEPTION: Stable, dry, intact eschar on heels — monitor only.

Unstageable.nutrition: High-protein diet, supplementation as for Stage 3/4
Unstageable.monitoring: Reassess after debridement to determine true stage
Unstageable.referral: Wound care specialist consult for debridement planning

INFECTION_SIGNS: Increased pain or tenderness at wound site
Purulent drainage (pus) — yellow, green, or brown
